label: 26_brotherhood_morsi_egypts_egyptians
description: The conflict between the Muslim Brotherhood and non-Islamist Egyptians escalated after President Morsi's controversial decisions and subsequent trial. The crisis culminated in a military coup against Morsi, marking a significant shift in Egyptian politics.

earliest_articles.title: Threaten but Participate: Why Election Boycotts Are a Bad Idea
earliest_articles.excerpt: For a while, the run-up to the 2010 general elections in Iraq appeared to be déjà vu all over again. The National Dialogue Front (NDF), a key Sunni political party, had decided to pull out of the election to protest the disqualification of hundreds of candidates—most notably their leader, Salah al-Mutlaq—for alleged…

earliest_articles.title: Myanmar Boycott Is Misguided
earliest_articles.excerpt: On the face of it, it’s easy to support the courageous decision of the beleaguered Burmese opposition to boycott upcoming parliamentary elections in Myanmar (formerly Burma), even though the decision led to the dissolution of its National League for Democracy party. The elections are the first to be held since 1990,…

earliest_articles.title: Can’t We All Just Get Along?
earliest_articles.excerpt: Egyptian voters cast their ballots Tuesday for the Shura council, the country’s upper house of Parliament, amidst widespread allegations of vote-rigging and outright government violence and intimidation. Even by Egypt’s low standards, the polls marked a tightening of political space for the opposition. The regime’s…

newest_articles.title: Hosni Mubarak’s risk-averse reign brought Egypt to calamity
newest_articles.excerpt: uprising. In November, 2010, Mubarak held elections for the lower house of parliament, again with levels of fraud and intimidation far higher than the previous years. newest_articles.title: The tragedy of Egypt’s Mohamed Morsi
newest_articles.excerpt: Though deeply flawed, Mohamed Morsi was a product of Egypt’s brief experiment with democracy, writes Shadi Hamid. His memory highlights what the country has lost. This piece was originally published in the Atlantic. Mohamed Morsi’s life, especially his later life, was the product of a series of accidents. When I first…

newest_articles.title: What does Mohammed Morsi’s death mean for Egypt?
newest_articles.excerpt: Egypt under the el-Sissi regime is a far cry from the period of flawed, but real democratic process that marked Mohammed Morsi’s election as Egypt’s president in 2012. Following Morsi’s sudden death in a Cairo court, Shadi Hamid evaluates his legacy and how his passing symbolizes international and regional resignation…

central_articles.title: Is There an Egyptian Nation?
central_articles.excerpt: In the latest round of Egypt’s current crisis — once again pitting Islamists against non-Islamists — demonstrators gathered at the presidential palace in Cairo to protest President Mohamed Morsi’s stunning decision to claim authoritarian, albeit temporary powers and his subsequent moves to rush through a controversial…

central_articles.title: The Trial of the Muslim Brotherhood
central_articles.excerpt: On November 4, the ousted former president of Egypt, Mohammed Morsi, along with 14 other members of the Muslim Brotherhood, was put on trial for incitement to murder. Twice in two hours, it was adjourned. It is the second time an Egyptian president has been put on trial in two years. However, whereas the trial of…

central_articles.title: Was Mohammed Morsi Really an Autocrat?
central_articles.excerpt: In the months leading up to Egypt’s military coup on July 3, 2013, it became common to hear some variation of the following: President Mohammed Morsi was a new pharaoh, a dictator in the making, or a purveyor of a new, dangerous kind of fascism.
